# Visualization Design Report

This report documents the design decisions behind the AircraftVerse Design Explorer dashboard.

## Design Overview

The project started with the goal of exploring how design parameters influence simulated aircraft performance. During implementation, the concept was refined from a broad exploratory dashboard into a guided analysis tool for aviation students.

The final dashboard is structured around four views:

- **Viability Landscape**: shows whether designs are viable at all.
- **Design Families**: compares interpretable aircraft design families.
- **High-Performing Recipes**: identifies repeated design combinations with strong performance.
- **Free Explorer**: supports open-ended exploration.

This structure matches the main user need: aviation students should be able to understand design-performance trade-offs without reading raw simulation files.

## Data-To-Visual Mapping

: Overview of main visual encodings. {#tbl-encoding-overview}

| View / Chart | Data variables | Mark type | Visual channels | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flight status counts | `flight_status`, design count | Bar | x-position, bar length, semantic colour | categorical x, linear y |
| Hub family success plot | `fly_rate`, `hover_rate`, design count, hub family | Point | x/y position, point size, colour hue | linear x/y, categorical colour |
| Metric distributions | distance, hover time, mass | Histogram | x-position, bar length | linear x, count y |
| Family ranking | selected family dimension, p90/median metrics | Bar | categorical position, length, colour intensity | categorical x, linear y |
| Mass vs long-range potential | median mass, p90 distance, hover rate, group size | Point | x/y position, point size, colour scale | linear x/y |
| Recipe comparison | recipe hover rate, median distance, group size | Point and table | x/y position, size, colour, tabular values | linear x/y |
| Pareto trade-off | distance, hover time, speed, Pareto status | Point | x/y position, point size, colour | linear x/y |
| Free explorer | user-selected variables | Point and table | x/y position, colour, hover details | mixed |

## Layout And Composition

The dashboard uses tabs rather than a long scrolling page. This keeps each analytical task focused and helps users navigate between overview, comparison and exploration.

Global filters are placed in the sidebar:

- flight status
- hub family
- battery cell count
- mass range

These filters coordinate all views. This lets users move from a broad overview to a focused subset without changing pages or rebuilding charts manually.

## Interaction Design

The main interactions are:

- **Filtering**: users narrow the dataset by flight status, hub family, battery cells and mass.
- **Tabs**: users switch between analytical tasks.
- **Hover tooltips**: users inspect exact values and design identifiers.
- **Expandable glossary**: users can decode aviation and dataset terms when needed.
- **Free explorer controls**: users choose x-axis, y-axis, colour variable and sample size.

These interactions support the aviation-student persona by reducing the need to understand raw technical variable names before exploring the data.

## Visual Style And Accessibility

The visual style uses a dark application background with light chart surfaces. Charts use Plotly defaults and restrained colour palettes to keep attention on data rather than decoration. Semantic colours are used where appropriate, for example to distinguish hover-capable designs from non-viable designs.

Important accessibility and readability choices:

- human-readable labels are used for raw generator categories where possible
- the glossary explains technical terms
- grouped statistics are shown in tables as well as charts
- charts use labelled axes and hover details
- percentile-clipped plotting values reduce distortion from extreme outliers

## Design Rationale

The dataset contains many designs with zero performance. A dashboard that only shows top aircraft would hide this important structure. Therefore the first view focuses on viability.

The data also contains many technical design parameters. For the target audience, family-level comparisons are more understandable than raw JSON-level variables. The dashboard therefore emphasizes hub families, battery cell count, fuselage type and wing presence.

The recipe view was chosen to avoid over-interpreting individual outliers. A recipe is only shown as important when it appears in enough designs to support a group-level conclusion.

## Known Limitations

- The dashboard uses simulated data, not physical flight test data.
- Some generator terms still need domain interpretation.
- The dashboard does not perform predictive modelling.
- The free explorer samples points for performance and readability when many records are visible.

## Conclusion

The final visualization design supports the project objectives by giving users an overview of aircraft viability, tools for comparing design families, and a way to inspect high-performing trade-offs. It balances guided interpretation with open-ended exploration.
